title: Integer types
instruction:
The most common integer is `i32`. Rust also has `i8`, `i16`, `i64`, `i128` and unsigned versions `u8`..`u128`.

Run this.
successMessage: Each integer type has a range. Use the smallest type that fits. The _ separator is purely visual — 1_000_000 equals 1000000.

title: Floating-point
instruction:
`f64` (double precision) is the default float type. Use `f32` only when memory is critical.

Run this.
successMessage: {:.4} formats to 4 decimal places. f64 is the workhorse float for most Rust programs.

title: Integer arithmetic
instruction:
Standard operators: `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `%`. Integer division truncates.

Run this.
successMessage: 10 / 3 = 3 in integer division (truncated). The remainder operator % gives 1.

title: Boolean type
instruction:
A `bool` is `true` or `false`. Comparison operators produce booleans.

Run this.
successMessage: ! negates a boolean. &&, ||, ! are the logical operators. Booleans are the fuel for all control flow.

title: The char type
instruction:
A `char` uses single quotes and stores a single Unicode scalar value — including emoji.

Run this.
successMessage: Rust chars are 4 bytes and store any Unicode code point. 'z' as u32 gives its numeric value 122.

title: Type conversion with as
instruction:
Rust never converts types implicitly. Use `as` to convert between numerics.

Note: converting float to int *truncates*, it does not round.

Run this.
successMessage: 3.99 as i32 becomes 3 — truncation, not rounding. as is explicit, so you always know a conversion is happening.

title: Overflow behaviour
instruction:
In debug mode, integer overflow panics. In release mode it wraps.

Use checked operations for safe arithmetic:

Run this.
successMessage: checked_add returns None on overflow. saturating_add clamps to MAX. These prevent silent data corruption.

title: Numeric methods
instruction:
Numbers have built-in methods. Explore a few.

Run this.
successMessage: abs, pow, sqrt — numeric methods make math expressive. i32::MIN is an associated constant giving the smallest i32 value.
